sections.title: Lesson Summary (பாடச் சுருக்கம்)
sections.content: The story 'On Monday Morning' revolves around a mischievous young boy named Tom who hates going to school on Monday mornings. To skip school and go fishing, he decides to pretend he is sick. After a failed attempt at faking a stomach ache, he focuses on his sore toe and begins groaning loudly to wake his sleeping half-brother, Sid. Tom acts as if he is dying, forgiving everyone and leaving his belongings (a one-eyed cat and a window-sash) to a new girl in town. Terrified, Sid fetches Aunt Polly. Though initially panicked, Aunt Polly quickly realizes Tom is faking it when he claims his 'toe is dying'. She laughs and cries simultaneously out of relief. Feeling foolish, Tom switches his excuse to a loose tooth. Unfortunately for him, Aunt Polly calls his bluff and uses a silk thread and a chunk of fire from the kitchen to pull the tooth out. Tom's plan fails, and he loses a tooth in the process.

sections.title: Important Concepts: Tom's Master Plan (பாடக்கருத்துக்கள்)
sections.content: Monday mornings always found Tom miserable because it meant the beginning of another slow week of school. He lay in bed wishing he was sick. He investigated his body for symptoms. He initially felt a stomach ache, but it grew feeble and died away. He then decided to use his sore toe as an excuse. He started groaning with considerable spirit, hoping to wake Sid, but Sid slept on unconsciously.

sections.title: Important Concepts: Waking Sid and Faking Death (பாடக்கருத்துக்கள்)
sections.content: Since groaning didn't work, Tom shook Sid, which successfully woke him up. Tom continued to groan pitifully. Sid became anxious and asked what was wrong. Tom dramatically asked Sid not to shake him and claimed he had been suffering for 'hours'. Playing the part of a dying person perfectly, Tom forgave Sid for everything and instructed him to give his window-sash and his one-eyed cat to the 'new girl that's come to town'. Believing Tom was truly dying, Sid rushed downstairs to inform Aunt Polly.

sections.title: Important Concepts: Aunt Polly's Reaction (பாடக்கருத்துக்கள்)
sections.content: When Sid told Aunt Polly that Tom was dying, her immediate response was 'Rubbish! I don't believe it!' However, her maternal instincts kicked in; her face grew white, and her lip trembled. She rushed upstairs with Sid and Mary at her heels. When she anxiously asked Tom what was wrong, he replied, 'Oh, auntie, my sore toe's dying!' Realizing the absurdity of a 'dying toe', Aunt Polly sank into a chair, laughed a little, and then cried a little. She scolded him to stop the nonsense.

sections.title: Important Concepts: The Tooth Extraction (பாடக்கருத்துக்கள்)
sections.content: With his toe excuse busted, Tom quickly changed his strategy and complained about an awful aching loose tooth. He regretted it immediately when Aunt Polly asked Mary to bring a 'silk thread' and a 'chunk of fire' from the kitchen. Realizing she was going to pull it out, Tom begged her to stop, admitting he just wanted to stay home from school and go fishing. Ignoring his pleas, Aunt Polly tied one end of the silk thread to his tooth and the other to the bedpost. She then suddenly pushed the chunk of fire almost into his face. The sudden jerk caused the tooth to be pulled out, leaving it hanging loosely by the bedpost.

sections.title: Glossary (கலைச்சொற்கள்)
sections.content:
• Panting - breathing quickly
• Exertions - effort
• Aggravated - irritated
• Snort - a cry made to show unhappiness
• Anxiously - tensely
• Loop - curve
• Chunk - a thick, solid piece of something

sections.title: Book Back 1-Mark Questions (பாட பின்புற வினாக்கள்)
sections.content:
▸ A. Name the speaker:
• 'No never mind. It’ll be over by and by, maybe. Don’t call anybody.' - Tom
• 'It makes my flesh crawl to hear you. What is the matter?' - Sid
• 'Your tooth indeed! What’s the matter with your tooth?' - Aunt Polly

▸ B. True or False:
• Tom enjoyed Monday mornings as he had to go to school. - False
• Tom’s first groan woke up Sid from his sound sleep. - False
• Tom wanted to give his dog with one eye to the new girl who had come to town. - False (It was a cat)
• Aunt Polly sent Sid to fetch the dentist. - False (She pulled it herself)

▸ E. Choose the correct answer:
• Tom pretended his toe was dying in order to - a. miss school.
• Aunt Polly pulled Tom’s tooth out with - c. a piece of thread.
• Tom was miserable on Monday morning because - b. he hated going to school.

sections.title: Vocabulary and Grammar (தகவல் பேழை)
sections.content:
▸ Relationship Terms:
• Siblings - brothers and sisters
• Kith and kin - friends and relatives
• A chip of the old block - a person who is very similar to their mother or father in character or appearance
• Two peas in a pod - two people who are very similar in appearance or character
• Bread winner - a person who earns money to support a family
• Forefathers - ancestors

▸ Riddle Questions:
• X and Y are parents to Z. But Z is not the son of X. Then what is Z to X? - Daughter.
• Meera’s brother is the father of Aakash. Then how is Aakash related to Meera? - Nephew (Meera is the aunt).

sections.title: Do You Know? (உங்களுக்கு தெரியுமா?)
sections.content: This story is an excerpt from the famous novel 'The Adventures of Tom Sawyer' written by Mark Twain, an American writer, humorist, and essayist. Mark Twain's real name was Samuel Langhorne Clemens.

sections.title: Remember (நினைவில் கொள்க)
sections.content:
• Tom's First Excuse: Stomach ache (failed because the pain went away).
• Tom's Second Excuse: Sore toe 'dying' (failed because Aunt Polly found it ridiculous).
• Tom's Third Excuse: Loose tooth (backfired because Aunt Polly pulled it out using a silk thread and a chunk of fire).
• Tom's true intention: To skip school and go fishing.

questions.question: Why did Tom find Monday mornings miserable?
options: He had to wake up early for fishing.
He hated the breakfast prepared by Aunt Polly.
It meant the beginning of another week of school.
He had to do chores around the house.
questions.explanation: Tom disliked Monday mornings because it signaled the start of a long week of school, which he hated.

questions.question: What was the first illness Tom thought of faking?
questions.explanation: When Tom examined himself, the first symptom he thought he detected was a stomach ache.

questions.question: Why did Tom give up on the idea of faking a stomach ache?
options: He forgot how to act like his stomach hurt.
Aunt Polly already knew he ate too many apples.
Sid told him it wasn't believable.
The ache soon grew feeble and died wholly away.
questions.explanation: He abandoned the stomach ache excuse because the imaginary pain faded away quickly.

questions.question: Which body part did Tom eventually choose to pretend was in severe pain?
questions.explanation: After the stomach ache idea failed, he decided to use his sore toe to seek attention.

questions.question: Who was sleeping peacefully while Tom was groaning?
questions.explanation: Sid was sleeping unconsciously in the same room while Tom started his dramatic groans.

questions.question: How did Tom finally manage to wake Sid up?
options: By throwing a pillow at him.
By shouting Sid's name and shaking him.
questions.explanation: When groaning failed, Tom was aggravated. He said, 'Sid, Sid!' and physically shook him.

questions.question: What did Tom tell Sid not to do when Sid woke up anxiously?
questions.explanation: Playing the victim, Tom moaned, 'Oh, don’t, Sid. Don’t shake me,' as if he was in fragile physical condition.

questions.question: How long did Tom claim he had been suffering when Sid asked?
questions.explanation: Tom exaggerated his suffering and replied 'Hours' when Sid asked how long he had been that way.

questions.question: What did Sid mean when he said, 'It makes my flesh crawl to hear you'?
options: He felt a physical itch on his skin.
He felt terrified and deeply uncomfortable.
questions.explanation: 'Makes my flesh crawl' is an idiom meaning to feel intense disgust, fear, or discomfort due to something creepy or scary.

questions.question: What 'dramatic' action did Tom do to convince Sid he was dying?
options: He forgave Sid and everyone for everything they had done.
He wrote a will on a piece of paper.
He stopped breathing for a minute.
questions.explanation: Tom played the dying scene perfectly by saying, 'I forgive you for everything, Sid... I forgive everybody.'

questions.question: To whom did Tom want to leave his 'window-sash' and 'cat with one eye'?
options: That new girl that's come to town
questions.explanation: Tom dramatically bequeathed his treasured items (window-sash and a one-eyed cat) to the new girl in town.

questions.question: What was Sid's immediate reaction after hearing Tom's 'dying' requests?
options: He snatched his clothes and ran downstairs to get Aunt Polly.
He started laughing at Tom's bad acting.
He tried to give Tom some medicine.
questions.explanation: Completely tricked, Sid snatched his clothes and flew downstairs to call Aunt Polly.

questions.question: What did Sid shout to Aunt Polly when he ran downstairs?
options: Oh, Aunt Polly, come! Tom's crying!
Oh, Aunt Polly, come! Tom's dying!
Oh, Aunt Polly, come! Tom has a fever!
Oh, Aunt Polly, come! Tom won't wake up!
questions.explanation: Sid panicked and screamed that Tom was dying.

questions.question: What was Aunt Polly's very first verbal response to Sid's news?
questions.explanation: Her immediate verbal reaction was disbelief: 'Rubbish! I don't believe it!'

questions.question: Despite her disbelief, what physical reaction showed Aunt Polly was actually scared?
options: Her face grew white and her lip trembled.
questions.explanation: The text states: 'And her face grew white, too, and her lip trembled,' showing her hidden panic.

questions.question: Who followed Aunt Polly as she fled upstairs?
questions.explanation: She fled upstairs with Sid and Mary at her heels.

questions.question: When Aunt Polly reached the bed, what ridiculous claim did Tom make?
questions.explanation: Tom foolishly claimed, 'Oh, auntie, my sore toe's dying!'

questions.question: How did Aunt Polly react upon hearing that Tom's 'toe was dying'?
options: She sank into a chair, laughed a little, and cried a little.
She slapped Tom for playing a prank.
questions.explanation: Relieved that it was just a silly prank, she sank down, laughed, and cried simultaneously.

questions.question: Why did Tom's groans stop suddenly?
options: The pain actually vanished, and he felt a little foolish because Aunt Polly caught his lie.
questions.explanation: Once his trick was exposed and Aunt Polly told him to shut up that nonsense, the 'pain' vanished, and he felt foolish.

questions.question: What was the second excuse Tom used after the toe plan failed?
questions.explanation: Trying to salvage his plan to stay home, he shifted the focus to his loose tooth.

questions.question: According to Tom, how badly did his loose tooth ache?
options: It felt like a small pinch.
questions.explanation: Tom exaggerated and said, 'One of them is loose, and it aches perfectly awful.'

questions.question: What two items did Aunt Polly ask Mary to bring from the kitchen?
options: A pair of pliers and a towel
A silk thread and a chunk of fire
A piece of wire and cold ice
questions.explanation: Aunt Polly commanded: 'Mary, get me a silk thread, and a chunk of fire out of the kitchen.'

questions.question: What did Tom realize when Aunt Polly asked for the thread and fire?
options: She was going to warm his room.
She was going to stitch his clothes.
She was going to pull out his loose tooth.
She was going to cook food.
questions.explanation: He realized she intended to perform a painful homemade tooth extraction.

questions.question: What was Tom's real motive behind all the drama, which he finally confessed?
options: He wanted to play with his cat.
He wanted to stay home from school and go fishing.
He was afraid of a test at school.
questions.explanation: Aunt Polly deduced and Tom's plea confirmed: 'So all this row was because you thought you’d get to stay home from school and go fishing?'

questions.question: Where did Aunt Polly tie the other end of the silk thread?
questions.explanation: She tied one end to Tom's tooth and the other end to the bedpost.

questions.question: How did the chunk of fire help in pulling the tooth out?
options: Aunt Polly pushed it near Tom's face, making him jerk back out of fear, which pulled the tooth.
Tom blew on it, which caused the tooth to pop out.
questions.explanation: She thrust the fire near his face, causing an involuntary reflex. He jerked back, and since the tooth was tied to the bedpost, it popped out.

questions.question: What does the word 'exertions' mean in the glossary?
questions.explanation: According to the glossary, 'exertions' means effort.

questions.question: Choose the correct synonym for 'panting'.
questions.explanation: Panting means taking short, quick breaths (breathing quickly) after physical effort.

questions.question: What does 'aggravated' mean?
questions.explanation: The glossary defines 'aggravated' as irritated.

questions.question: Which word from the text means 'a cry made to show unhappiness'?
questions.explanation: A 'snort' is defined in the glossary as a cry made to show unhappiness or contempt.

questions.question:
Match the following relationship terms:
1. Siblings
2. Forefathers
3. Bread winner

A. Ancestors
B. Earner of the family
C. Brothers and sisters
questions.explanation: Siblings are brothers/sisters (C), Forefathers are ancestors (A), and Bread winner is the family earner (B).

questions.question: What is the meaning of the idiom 'a chip of the old block'?
options: A person who is very similar to their mother or father in character.
Two people who look exactly identical.
questions.explanation: This idiom refers to a child who closely resembles their parent in behavior or physical appearance.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the idiom 'two peas in a pod'?
options: Two people who hate each other.
Two people who are very similar in appearance or character.
questions.explanation: The idiom is used to describe two people who are extremely similar to each other.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Tom gave his one-eyed cat to Aunt Polly.
Reason (R): Tom wanted to make Aunt Polly happy before he 'died'.
options: A is true, R is false
A is false, R is true
Both A and R are true
Both A and R are false
questions.explanation: Tom wanted Sid to give the cat to the new girl in town, not Aunt Polly. Thus, both statements are completely false.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Aunt Polly did not call a dentist to pull Tom's tooth.
Reason (R): She knew a traditional homemade method using a thread and fire.
options: A is false, R is true
Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation
Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation
A is true, R is false
questions.explanation: She didn't need a dentist because she confidently used a classic home remedy to extract the loose tooth.

questions.question: Analogy: Stomach ache : Feeble :: Sore toe : ?
questions.explanation: Tom claimed his stomach ache grew feeble, while he claimed his sore toe was 'dying'.

questions.question: From the riddle section: X and Y are parents to Z. But Z is not the son of X. What is Z to X?
questions.explanation: If Z is a child of the parents but not a son, Z must be the daughter.

questions.question: From the riddle section: Meera’s brother is the father of Aakash. How is Aakash related to Meera?
questions.explanation: The child of one's brother is their nephew.

questions.question: HOTS Question: Why did Tom's imagination produce 'genuine tone' groans?
options: Because his acting and imagination were so intense that he almost convinced himself he was in pain.
Because he was practicing for a school play.
questions.explanation: The text says 'Tom was suffering in reality, now, his imagination was working well...' Meaning he got so absorbed in his lie that it started feeling real to him.

questions.question: HOTS Question: What does Aunt Polly's reaction (laughing and crying at the same time) reveal about her relationship with Tom?
options: She hates him and enjoys his suffering.
She loves him deeply, is constantly worried about him, but is also exasperated by his constant mischief.
She doesn't care whether he lives or dies.
questions.explanation: Her simultaneous tears (from sheer relief and worry) and laughter (at the absurdity of the prank) show her deep maternal love mixed with frustration.

questions.question: Life-situation Question: Tom lied to avoid going to school and ended up losing a tooth painfully. What is the moral lesson here?
options: Never trust your aunt with a fire chunk.
Lying to escape responsibilities often leads to worse consequences than facing the truth.
If you fake an illness, pick a better body part.
Always sleep deeply like Sid to avoid trouble.
questions.explanation: Tom's lie backfired terribly. He not only had to go to school eventually but also suffered a painful tooth extraction because of his own deceit.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the word 'chunk'?
options: A thick, solid piece of something
questions.explanation: A chunk means a thick, solid piece (in this case, a glowing piece of coal or wood from the kitchen fire).

questions.question: Who says this line? 'Tom, why didn’t you wake me sooner? Oh, Tom, DON’T!'
questions.explanation: Sid says this when he wakes up terrified, believing Tom has been dying for hours.

questions.question: Who says this line? 'Oh, please, auntie, don’t pull it out. It won’t hurt any more.'
questions.explanation: Tom says this in a panic when he realizes Aunt Polly is about to perform an extraction.

questions.question: In the phrase 'Aunt Polly, it seemed dying, and it hurt so I never minded my tooth at all', what does Tom try to do?
options: He tries to show off his bravery.
He tries to justify his lie by claiming the 'toe pain' distracted him from the real 'tooth pain'.
He blames Aunt Polly for the pain.
questions.explanation: Tom tries to smoothly transition his excuse from the fake toe to the loose tooth by claiming the toe hurt so much it made him forget the tooth.

questions.question: Book back question: 'Tom was miserable on Monday morning because _________'
questions.explanation: This is a direct question from the book back section. He hated going to school.

questions.question: Book back question: 'Aunt Polly pulled Tom’s tooth out with _________'
questions.explanation: She used a silk thread (and fire) to pull it out.

questions.question: What role does Mary play in the story?
options: She is the new girl in town.
She is the one who ran downstairs to call Aunt Polly.
She is an assistant who brings the thread and fire for Aunt Polly.
questions.explanation: Mary acts as Aunt Polly's helper, fetching the required items from the kitchen.

questions.question:
Arrange the events in order:
1. Aunt Polly extracts the tooth.
2. Tom fakes a stomach ache.
3. Sid runs to Aunt Polly.
4. Tom fakes a dying toe.
questions.explanation: First, stomach ache (2). Then, dying toe (4). Then, Sid runs to Aunt (3). Finally, the tooth is extracted (1).

questions.question: What is the final state of Tom's tooth at the end of the narrative?
options: It is fixed perfectly back in his mouth.
It is hanging loosely by the bedpost.
It falls out of the window.
questions.explanation: The text ends with: 'The tooth was hanging loosely by the bedpost, now.'
